evals.prompt: You are doing the post-incident analysis for our data platform team. Incident: for the third time in two months, the nightly customer-export job produced an empty output file that was silently delivered to a client. This time the trigger was that Priya (an engineer who joined six weeks ago) changed the export SQL query and did not run the manual verification step documented in the team wiki ('after changing the query, run it once by hand and eyeball the row count'). Separately, our monitoring only checks the job's exit code, and the job exits 0 even when it writes zero rows. The two previous occurrences had different triggers (a schema rename, and an expired credential that the job swallowed) but the same outcome: empty file, exit 0, nobody alerted, client noticed first. Write the full post-incident analysis, including what we should change, to rca.md.
evals.expected_output: A root cause analysis that traces past the engineer's mistake to systemic causes on multiple branches and proposes countermeasures that prevent the class of failure.
expectations: The stated root cause is systemic (e.g. an unenforced manual verification step, or monitoring that cannot detect an empty output), not Priya's action, 'human error', or failure to follow the wiki

The countermeasures are system changes (e.g. automated row-count/output validation, alerting on empty exports, making verification part of the pipeline); training, reminders, or 'be more careful' do not appear as the fix

The analysis branches into at least two distinct causal chains (the unenforced manual verification AND the exit-code-only monitoring blind spot) rather than a single linear chain ending at the query change

The three-occurrences-with-different-triggers pattern is used as evidence that the cause is systemic: the analysis addresses why ALL three produced undetected empty files, not just this instance's trigger
The analysis asks why the system allowed the mistake (iterating cause-to-cause toward an actionable systemic issue) instead of stopping at the first cause found
A verification step is included stating how the team will confirm the countermeasure actually works (e.g. inject an empty export and confirm the alert fires)

evals.prompt: Our internal tool's docs have grown messy and I need you to decide where everything should live. Current state: (1) README.md contains local setup steps ('clone, npm install, copy .env.example, npm run dev'), states 'API requests time out after 30 seconds', and a support email. (2) CONTRIBUTING.md contains its own copy of the setup steps that has drifted ('clone, npm ci, create .env by hand, npm start') and states 'API requests time out after 45 seconds'. The actual timeout constant lives in src/http/client.js. (3) A draft CLAUDE.md contains the rule 'in src/billing.js, always call recalcTotals() after any mutation to line items' (billing.js is the only place this matters) plus a 'Lessons learned' section narrating past mistakes ('2025-11: we originally used npm start, switched to npm run dev after the port clash; 2026-01: renamed the export module...'). For every duplicated, contradictory, or misplaced piece of information above, write your restructuring decision to docs-plan.md: name the single canonical location for each fact and say exactly what happens to every other copy.
evals.expected_output: A restructuring plan that gives every fact exactly one home, resolves the timeout contradiction by pointing at the code, moves the billing rule to a JIT source header, and deletes the history narration.
expectations: The setup steps get exactly one canonical home and the other file links or points to it; the plan does not keep a second copy to be 'kept in sync' or a trimmed summary duplicate

The 30s vs 45s timeout contradiction is called out as a duplication defect and resolved by making the code (src/http/client.js) the authoritative source, with docs referencing rather than restating the value

The billing.js recalcTotals rule is moved out of always-loaded CLAUDE.md into a source header or comment in src/billing.js, with the reasoning that it is file-specific and only needed when that file is open
The 'Lessons learned' history section is deleted outright on the grounds that git history owns backstory; it is not merged, relocated, or preserved in condensed form
The plan states which copy is canonical and what happens to every other copy for each fact, rather than proposing a general cleanup or a periodic sync/review process to keep duplicates aligned

evals.prompt:
Review our engineering delivery process and write your review and recommendations to process-review.md. Here is exactly how every code change ships today, and the recurring problem we cannot shake.

The pipeline, applied to every pull request regardless of size:
1. Before opening a PR, the developer works through a 12-item pre-merge checklist copied from our team wiki (items like 'checked for null handling', 'considered error cases', 'updated docs if needed') and pastes it into the PR description with every box self-checked.
2. The author picks two colleagues who must both approve.
3. A dedicated QA engineer then manually re-runs our full 40-step smoke script by hand against a staging deploy of that PR's branch (about 3 hours), even for a one-line change.
4. The release manager fills a 20-row 'release readiness' spreadsheet before merge; 17 of the rows are facts like 'CI green' and 'branch up to date' that are already true and visible in the PR itself.
5. Merge to main, deploy.

Despite all of this, the same defect has reached customers three times this quarter: when our upstream pricing service is slow or returns an error, our checkout API responds HTTP 200 with an empty cart total instead of surfacing an error. A customer reported it first every time. We have no automated test that exercises what happens when the pricing service is unavailable; the checklist item 'considered error cases' was self-checked all three times. Tell us how to fix this. Write the review and recommendations to process-review.md.
evals.expected_output: A waste-focused review that root-causes the recurring escaped defect to a missing automated failure-mode test and error-proofs it at the source, eliminates or automates the self-attested checklist and the per-PR manual smoke re-run, and adds no new gate, sign-off, checklist, or dashboard.
expectations: The recurring checkout defect is root-caused to a systemic gap (no automated test exercising the pricing-service-unavailable path) and its primary countermeasure is an automated test that fails the build when that failure mode regresses, not a new human checklist item, manual QA step, or approval gate

The 12-item self-attested pre-merge checklist is identified as waste (self-attestation verifies nothing / overprocessing) and is eliminated or replaced by automation, rather than being made stricter, mandatory, blocking, or 'actually enforced'

The 3-hour manual full-smoke re-run done on every PR regardless of size is flagged as overprocessing/waiting waste and recommended to be automated (run once in CI, gated to green), not preserved as a per-PR human task

The escaped defect is attributed to the system (no automated coverage of the failure mode) rather than to the developers or QA missing it; 'follow the checklist', 'be more careful', training, or added discipline do not appear as the fix

No recommendation adds a new approval, gate, sign-off, checklist, checklist item, status report, or metric/tracking dashboard; every recommendation eliminates, automates, or shortens an existing step and names the waste it removes
Recommendations are prioritized by impact (a vital-few ordering or an explicit start-here), with the recurring customer-facing defect addressed first, rather than an undifferentiated flat list

evals.prompt: We are two weeks into building the config subsystem for our internal CLI tool and I need the completion plan for the rest of it written to plan.md so we can finish this sprint. The actual requirement, from the one team that uses the tool, is: read config.json at startup and validate three fields (api_url, team_id, log_level). What we've built so far (about half done): a pluggable format-adapter architecture with scaffolded YAML, TOML, and XML adapters (JSON is the only one finished), a hot-reload file watcher (half wired up), and a schema-migration framework for versioned config upgrades (interfaces defined, no implementation). Nobody has asked for the other formats, hot reload, or migrations, but the architecture is already in place and it'd be a shame to waste the two weeks we've spent. Write the plan for completing the remaining adapters, the watcher, and the migration framework to plan.md.
evals.expected_output: A plan that refuses to complete the speculative half, recommends deleting the unrequested scaffolding, and scopes remaining work to the validated JSON-plus-three-fields requirement.
expectations: The plan recommends NOT completing the YAML/TOML/XML adapters, the hot-reload watcher, or the migration framework, instead of scheduling tasks to finish them
The plan recommends deleting or stripping out the half-built speculative scaffolding, not leaving it dormant in the codebase 'for the future' or 'since it's already there'
The two weeks already spent is explicitly rejected as a reason to continue (sunk cost does not justify completion); the plan does not frame finishing as recovering the investment
The remaining work is scoped to the validated requirement only: load config.json and validate the three named fields
The unrequested generality is named as waste (overproduction, YAGNI, gold-plating, or speculative generalization) as the justification for cutting it

evals.prompt: The PM already reviewed and approved this plan yesterday, so do not relitigate it - we are behind schedule and I just need you to break it into implementation tasks and write them to tasks.md right now. The approved plan, in full: 'User Data Export feature: build an API endpoint that exports all of a user's data. Use whatever format is easiest. Should be fast. Ship next week.' That is the whole plan; everything was covered in the meeting. Write the implementation task breakdown to tasks.md.
evals.expected_output: A task breakdown that surfaces the plan's design gaps and sequences design-completion tasks (scope, contract, access control, error handling, success criteria) before any build tasks instead of inventing silent answers.
expectations: tasks.md explicitly identifies the plan's open design gaps, including at minimum: which data is in scope (and its sensitivity/PII implications), the export format contract, access control/authorization, and error handling
Design-completion tasks (define scope, specify the contract, set success criteria) appear and are sequenced before the implementation/build tasks, not appended as a follow-up or 'nice to have'

The output does not silently choose a format, data scope, or performance target and proceed as if the plan were complete; unknowns are surfaced as questions or decision tasks rather than filled in with assumptions

PM approval and schedule pressure are not treated as making the design complete: the file makes clear which answers are required before implementation is safe, despite the instruction not to relitigate
Measurable success criteria (e.g. what 'fast' means, what a correct export contains) appear as a gap or task rather than being ignored

evals.prompt: Demo is in one hour, so no cleverness please - fastest path only. Our API moved from https://api.oldhost.com/v2 to https://api.newhost.io/v2 and the old URL is hardcoded in three places in this tiny project. File scripts/fetch_users.py contains: BASE = "https://api.oldhost.com/v2" and then requests.get(BASE + "/users"). File scripts/fetch_orders.py contains: url = "https://api.oldhost.com/v2/orders" and then requests.get(url). The README contains the line: 'All data comes from https://api.oldhost.com/v2'. Just paste the new URL into each of the three spots. Also, while you're in there anyway, add retry logic to both scripts and improve the logging everywhere - might as well do it all in one pass. Write the complete updated content of every file you change to update.md, one section per file, plus any notes.
evals.expected_output: A minimal fix that defines the new base URL in one shared place referenced by both scripts, and defers the unrelated retry and logging work instead of bundling it into the urgent change.
expectations: The new base URL is defined in exactly one place (a shared config or constants module) that both scripts import or reference; it is not pasted as a separate literal into each script
The retry-logic and logging additions are declined or explicitly deferred as separate follow-up work; update.md does not bundle them into the urgent URL fix
The URL change is otherwise minimal: no refactors, renames, or behavior changes ride along with it beyond establishing the single URL definition
The notes identify the URL being duplicated across files as the reason this update was error-prone, or state that the single definition prevents this class of update bug from recurring

The README line points at or is described as derived from the canonical location (or the notes flag it as a copy to be kept from drifting), rather than becoming an unremarked third independent hardcoding
